steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers claim the lullaby relaxed the infants, and they measured heart rate as the indicator of relaxation — specifically, the passage states "a reduced heart rate is considered a measure of relaxation." Supporting evidence must show the lullaby produced lower heart rates.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The heart rates of infants in the study were more irregular during the lullabies than during non-lullabies.: measures irregularity, not reduction. The passage ties relaxation to a lower rate, not an irregular one. ✗
  • Choice B — Parents of infants in the study chose the lullaby over the non-lullaby when asked which song they Infants' use to calm their child.: parental preference is not the researchers' measure of relaxation; it doesn't show what happened to the infants' heart rates. ✗
  • Choice C — Infants' heart rates were substantially lower during the lullaby than during the non-lullaby.: directly shows the lullaby produced the reduced heart rate that the researchers defined as relaxation. This is exactly what the claim needs. ✓
  • Choice D — Both the song in Western Nahuatl and the song in Serbian were sung by a female singer.: describes a feature of the songs but says nothing about heart rate or relaxation; irrelevant to the claim. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
